---
title: "Module 4: GitHub Collaboration"
author: "Deepak Tanwar"
format:
  revealjs:
    controls-layout: bottom-right
    controls-tutorial: true
    footer: "Introduction to Git & GitHub — SIB/UZH"
    theme: default
    highlight-style: github
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#F05032"
---

## Module 4: Moving to GitHub

Your local repository is powerful, but it's only on your machine. GitHub brings three things:

1. **Backup** — your history lives online
2. **Collaboration** — multiple people can contribute
3. **Public sharing** — others can use your code

First: connect securely. That's SSH.

---

## SSH Authentication — setup

SSH lets your machine authenticate to GitHub without passwords:

```bash
# Check for existing key
ls -al ~/.ssh

# Generate new key (if needed)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your.email@uzh.ch"

# Copy public key → paste into github.com/settings/keys
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

# Test connection
ssh -T git@github.com
```

::: {.callout-note}
The private key never leaves your machine. GitHub only ever sees the public key — that's why it's safe to share the public key, and the private key is not.
:::

---

## SSH troubleshooting

| Error | Likely cause | Solution |
|-------|---|---|
| `Permission denied` | Private key not found | Run `ssh-keygen` to create one |
| `Connection refused` | Firewall blocking | Try HTTPS instead: `git config --global url."https://".insteadOf git://` |
| `Agent refused operation` | SSH agent not running | `eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"` then `ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519` |
| Public key not on GitHub | Key not uploaded | Copy public key, paste in [github.com/settings/keys](https://github.com/settings/keys) |

::: {.callout-tip}
**If none of these work:** Try using HTTPS instead of SSH (`https://github.com/USERNAME/REPO.git`). HTTPS is simpler for first-time users and doesn't require SSH key setup.
:::

---

## Connecting local to remote

```bash
# Link your local repo to GitHub
git remote add origin git@github.com:USERNAME/REPO.git

# Verify
git remote -v
```

`origin` is just the conventional name for your primary remote.

---

## git push

Share your commits with GitHub:

```bash
# First time — push and set upstream
git push -u origin main

# Every time after
git push
```

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
`git push` only uploads **commits**. Uncommitted changes stay local.
:::

---

## git pull

Get the latest changes from GitHub:

```bash
git pull
```

Under the hood this is two steps:

```bash
git fetch    # download new commits
git merge    # merge them into your branch
```

. . .

**Run `git pull` at the start of every work session.**

---

## git clone

Copy an entire repository to your machine:

```bash
git clone git@github.com:DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github.git

# Into a custom folder name
git clone git@github.com:DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github.git my-folder
```

Clone includes the full history and sets up `origin` automatically.

---

## The full remote workflow

Every command you'll actually use them, in order:

```bash
git clone <url>           # 1. get the repo (first time only)
git checkout -b feature   # 2. work on a branch
# make changes...
git add --all             # 3. stage
git commit -m "message"   # 4. commit
git push origin feature   # 5. push to GitHub
# 6. open Pull Request on GitHub
# 7. after merge: git checkout main && git pull
```

This is the complete workflow for collaborating on GitHub.

---

## Pull Request workflow

Visual guide to the Fork → Branch → PR → Merge cycle:

```{mermaid}
graph LR
    A["Your Fork<br/>on GitHub"] -->|git clone| B["Local Machine"]
    B -->|git checkout -b feature| C["Feature Branch"]
    C -->|make changes| D["Committed Changes"]
    D -->|git push origin feature| E["Push to Fork"]
    E -->|Create PR| F["Pull Request<br/>on Main Repo"]
    F -->|Code Review| G{"Approved?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Merge to main"]
    G -->|No| C
    H -->|git pull| I["Update Your Local"]
    
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#f3e5f5
    style C fill:#e8f5e9
    style F fill:#fff3e0
    style H fill:#c8e6c9
    style I fill:#bbdefb
```

---

## GitHub Issues workflow

Track, assign, and close work:

```{mermaid}
graph TD
    A["🆕 Create Issue<br/>Title + Description"] --> B["📋 Issue Backlog"]
    B --> C["👤 Assign to yourself<br/>or teammate"]
    C --> D["🔖 Add labels<br/>bug, feature, docs, etc"]
    D --> E["🏗️ In Progress"]
    E --> F["💻 Create branch<br/>git checkout -b fix-X"]
    F --> G["✅ Commit work<br/>closes #issue-number"]
    G --> H["🔀 Create PR"]
    H --> I["✔️ Review & Merge"]
    I --> J["🎯 Issue Auto-closes"]
    
    style A fill:#ffebee
    style B fill:#fff9c4
    style E fill:#fff3e0
    style H fill:#e1f5fe
    style J fill:#c8e6c9
```

---

## Merge conflict resolution

Step-by-step visual guide:

```{mermaid}
graph TD
    A["Conflict: Two branches<br/>changed same lines"] --> B["❌ git merge fails"]
    B --> C["📖 Open conflicted file"]
    C --> D["See conflict markers:<br/><<<<<<< HEAD<br/>your changes<br/>=======<br/>their changes<br/>>>>>>>> branch-name"]
    D --> E{"Decide: Keep<br/>yours, theirs,<br/>or both?"}
    E -->|Keep yours| F["Delete their section"]
    E -->|Keep theirs| G["Delete your section"]
    E -->|Keep both| H["Keep both, remove markers"]
    F --> I["Remove conflict markers<br/><<<<<<< ======= >>>>>>>"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["git add resolved_file"]
    J --> K["git commit -m 'resolved conflict'"]
    K --> L["✅ Merge complete"]
    
    style A fill:#ffcdd2
    style B fill:#ffcdd2
    style D fill:#fff9c4
    style L fill:#c8e6c9
```

---

## GitHub Issues

Track tasks, bugs, and discussions — all in one place.

- **Bug report:** "Script crashes on paired-end input"
- **Feature request:** "Add support for BAM files"
- **Task:** "Write documentation for pipeline module"

. . .

Reference issues in commit messages:

```bash
git commit -m "fixed alignment bug, closes #12"
```

→ Automatically closes issue #12 when the PR is merged.

---

## GitHub Projects

Issues are individual cards. A Project is the board that organizes them.

```
| To Do      | In Progress | Done       |
|------------|-------------|------------|
| Issue #1   | Issue #3    | Issue #2   |
| Issue #4   |             |            |
```

Drag cards as work progresses.
Link issues so they move automatically when a PR closes them.

---

## Pull Requests

We planned the work with Issues and Projects. Now the actual code change:
a Pull Request is a proposal to merge your branch into another branch.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[your-branch] -->|Pull Request| B[main]
    B -->|review + discuss| A
    A -->|merge| B
```

**PR workflow:**
1. Push your branch: `git push origin my-feature`
2. Open PR on GitHub: **Compare & pull request**
3. Write a description: what changed, why, issue reference
4. Request a reviewer
5. Address review comments with new commits
6. Merge

---

## Fork vs Clone

| | Fork | Clone |
|--|------|-------|
| **Where** | GitHub (server copy) | Your machine (local copy) |
| **When** | Contributing to someone else's repo | Working on any repo locally |
| **Push to** | Your fork → PR to original | Original (if you have permission) |

**Fork workflow:**

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Fork on GitHub] --> B[Clone your fork]
    B --> C[Branch]
    C --> D[Edit & commit]
    D --> E[Push to your fork]
    E --> F[Pull Request to original]
```

---

## Exercise 5 — Push & Pull

See [Exercise 5](../exercises/exercise_5_push_pull.qmd)

1. Create GitHub repo (no README)
2. `git remote add origin <ssh-url>`
3. `git push -u origin main`
4. Edit README on GitHub → `git pull` locally
5. Edit locally → `git push` → check on GitHub

---

## Exercise 6 — Fork & Pull Request

See [Exercise 6](../exercises/exercise_6_collaboration.qmd)

1. Fork `DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github`
2. Clone your fork
3. `git checkout -b YOUR_FIRST_NAME`
4. Add your section to `projects.md`
5. `git push origin YOUR_FIRST_NAME`
6. Open a Pull Request → reference your issue

---

## You're done! 🎉

You've learned:

- **Why** version control matters (Module 1)
- **How** to use Git locally (Module 2)
- **How** to work safely with branches (Module 3)
- **How** to collaborate on GitHub (Module 4)

---

## Your new skills ✅

✅ Version-control your analysis and code  
✅ Try new approaches without breaking what works  
✅ Collaborate with colleagues  
✅ Contribute to open-source projects  

**Next:** These skills apply to everything. Take them forward.

---

**Questions?** 🙋
